summary: Gruber pays tribute to Sean Connery following his death at 90, arguing that Connery's greatness extended far beyond James Bond. He highlights Connery's method of understanding characters by studying what they don't know, as revealed in his preparation for The Untouchables. Gruber contrasts Connery's Bond with Roger Moore's, arguing that Connery's genius was making the audience believe Bond genuinely feared for his life, while Moore played Bond as someone who knew the script would save him. This vulnerability, combined with effortless cool, is what gives Connery's Bond films their lasting power.
key_points: Connery's career beyond Bond was remarkable, with indelible roles in films by Lumet, McTiernan, Spielberg, and De Palma
Connery's acting method involved reading scenes his character wasn't in to understand what the character doesn't know, avoiding the trap of 'playing information they don't have'
In The Untouchables, Connery's Malone believes the mission is futile but tries anyway because doing the right thing is what matters

The fundamental difference between Connery's and Moore's Bond is epistemic: Connery played Bond as a man who might not survive the next scene, while Moore winked at the audience knowing the script would save him
Connery's combination of genuine vulnerability and effortless cool is what gives his Bond films staying power
money_quotes: The trap that bad actors fall into is playing information they don't have.
Connery's Jim Malone thinks they won't, it's futile, but it's worth trying anyway because that's what you do: you do the right thing as best you can.
Connery makes you believe that he's a man who thinks he's about to be killed, crotch first, by a goddamn laser.
Moore's Bond winkingly knew what the audience knew — that the last page of the script had the world saved and Bond in embrace with the girl.
Connery's Bond felt like he didn't know if he'd survive to the next scene — that every fight might be his last.
That, combined with his effervescent cool, is what instills those movies with staying power.
Charm is actually not all that easy a quality to come by.
key_insight: Connery's genius as Bond was playing a man who genuinely believed he might die in every scene, and that authentic vulnerability combined with effortless cool is what separates timeless screen performances from mere charm.
